coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot cross-linked polyvinylpyrrolidone hybrid hydrogel for simultaneous dye adsorption, photodegradation and bacterial elimination from waste water
coredata.dc:creator: Nayak, Suman
Prasad, Surendra Rajit
Mandal, Debabrata
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: The creation of a polymeric hydrogel from polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) cross-linked by Carbon Quantum Dots (CD) for the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of both cationic and anionic dyes. PVP, an important biocompatible constituent and often surplus in cosmetic industry, was carboxylated through NaOH refluxing and covalently conjugated to surface amine functionality of CD derived from lemon juice and Cysteamine. The hybrid hydrogel was obtained from PVP-CD covalent conjugate by careful manipulation of pH and found to possess better rheological properties than only carboxylate-PVP. The monolayer physisorption of the dyes on the hydrogel was affected by hydrogen bonding, dispersion or inductive effect, and π-π interaction with the polymer backbone as well as the CD that followed pseudo-second-order kinetics. Degradation of the adsorbed dyes was instated by the unique Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generating ability of the CD embedded in the hydrogel matrix upon exposure to sunlight, the mechanism of which is also unveiled. The same CD-induced ROS was found to effectively annihilate both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria in real polluted water in less than 10 min of photoexcitation of the hydrogel. The hydrogel was restored by mild acid wash that is able to perform dye adsorption and photo-degradation upto four cycles.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot cross-linked polyvinylpyrrolidone hybrid hydrogel for simultaneous dye adsorption, photodegradation and bacterial elimination from waste water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The creation of a polymeric hydrogel from polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) cross-linked by Carbon Quantum Dots (CD) for the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of both cationic and anionic dyes. PVP, an important biocompatible constituent and often surplus in cosmetic industry, was carboxylated through NaOH refluxing and covalently conjugated to surface amine functionality of CD derived from lemon juice and Cysteamine. The hybrid hydrogel was obtained from PVP-CD covalent conjugate by careful manipulation of pH and found to possess better rheological properties than only carboxylate-PVP. The monolayer physisorption of the dyes on the hydrogel was affected by hydrogen bonding, dispersion or inductive effect, and π-π interaction with the polymer backbone as well as the CD that followed pseudo-second-order kinetics. Degradation of the adsorbed dyes was instated by the unique Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generating ability of the CD embedded in the hydrogel matrix upon exposure to sunlight, the mechanism of which is also unveiled. The same CD-induced ROS was found to effectively annihilate both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria in real polluted water in less than 10 min of photoexcitation of the hydrogel. The hydrogel was restored by mild acid wash that is able to perform dye adsorption and photo-degradation upto four cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Textile and manufacturing industries use more than 10,000 commercially available dyes leading to ∼50 K tonnes/year global discharge (Peng et al., 2016; Qi et al., 2019). Most of these dyes are discharged into the water as effluent, which is now a worldwide concern due to its impact on our ecosystem, including human health (Bianchini et al., 2015). Dye-containing wastewater is usually treated by physical or chemical techniques like electro-floatation, kinetic coagulation, membrane filtration, precipitation, irradiation, ozonization, flocculation, and Katox treatment involving activated carbon (Szpyrkowicz, 2005; Van der Bruggen et al., 2001; Zhu et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2006; Cano Quiroz et al., 2010; Malik, 2004). In particular, adsorption and subsequent photo-degradation of the dyes are one of the most effective and viable techniques for dye removal, considering the balance between efficiency and cost. However, the milieu of simultaneous dye adsorption on a well-formulated medium, its metal-free degradation, and elimination of microbes from wastewater using sustainable materials is an incontestable challenge that is seldom achieved.

Many polymer-based hydrogels derived from chitin, alginate, chitosan, Xanthan gum, PVA, poly (AA-co-HEMA), Polyacrylamide, poly (N-vinyl imidazole) and other natural and artificial polymers have been employed for dye removal (Rocher et al., 2010; Chatterjee et al., 2018; Mittal et al., 2014; Ilgin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Polyvinylpyrrolydine (PVP) blended or grafted with alginate, Acrylic acid (AA), methacrylic acid has been used to create hydrogels for dye removal (Inal and Erduran, 2015; Jin et al., 2006; Şolpan and Kölge, 2006). As such, PVP alone has been crosslinked to form hydrogels using crosslinkers like K2S2O8 and tetraallyl ammonium bromide for dye adsorption applications (Senkal et al., 2006). However, in spite of high biodegradability potential, PVP based hydrogels have not been able to impress in dye adsorption applications.

Often photocatalytic degradation of dyes is done with externally incorporated catalysts doped with metals or their complexes, nonmetals, and semiconductor materials (Jain and Shrivastava, 2008; Zhou et al., 2010; Vinodgopal and Kamat, 1995). They have their own merits and demerits from costing, metal-induced toxicity, catalyst regeneration, and scaling up perspectives. TiO2 decorated graphene nanogels, and biocomposite hydrogels have been shown to photodegrade dyes and antibiotics essentially through the photo-induced generation of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) (Kumar et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2013; Ren et al., 2018). Reportedly, clay nanocomposite hydrogel derived membrane displayed methylene blue adsorption and degradation by Fenton agent (Bao et al., 2014). However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no example of metal-free hydrogels that could simultaneously adsorb and degrade dyes in a comprehensive and sustainable formulation.

Carbon-based nanomaterials like Carbon Nanotube (CNT) and Graphene Oxide (GO) held great potential for dye adsorption as well as photodegradation applications. Carbon Dot (CD), the latest member of the Carbon nano-family, has also been applied for dye removal through adsorption and degradation mechanism. Dye removal was demonstrated with composite derived from positively charged inorganic layered double Hydroxides decorated with CD by virtue of Hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions (Nayab et al., 2014). Photocatalytic Degradation of dyes by Mg-N-Embedded CD in the presence of sunlight was reported (Bhati et al., 2018). Elsewhere, CD has been used as an initiator for polymerization of poly (4, 4’-diaminodiphenylmethane (PDDM)) meant for dye adsorption applications (Maruthapandi et al., 2018). It is thus imperative that surface functional groups on CD play a major role in the adsorption and photodegradation process. We hypothesize that such effects can be translated to formulations for bulk and practical usage where CD could be endowed with the dual role as a crosslinker in hydrogel as well as a photocatalyst. In this context, we believe CD-infused hybrid hydrogels hold great promise for wastewater treatment for their low cost and ease of synthesis, chemical versatility arising from surface functionality, stability along with their substrate-dependent transformation into ROS generating species that also hints towards microbial elimination in a parallel consequence.

Elimination of microbes, including bacteria from wastewater, mostly involves UV irradiation or treatment with hyper oxygenated chemical compounds, which often come with the baggage of occupational hazards and costs. CD that generate ROS with visible light irradiation could be a worthy alternative to UV and chemical treatment for removing bacteria from wastewater (Rabe et al., 2019). However, an effective formulation is required to ensure easy separation of the CD and its reuse; one possibility been covalent conjugation of CD with polymers that could prevent leaching of ROS generating CD in the water while guaranteeing effective elimination of bacteria through a polymeric hydrogel formulation.

Herein, for the first time, we demonstrate the construction of a PVP-CD hybrid that displays excellent dye adsorption and photodegradation ability along with high antibacterial activity in an inexpensive, workable hydrogel formulation. We illustrate the adsorption and photodegradation of both cationic (Malachite Green and Crystal violet) and anionic dye (Eosin Y) on hydrogel derived from covalent conjugation of PVP with CD (Scheme 1 ). CD was synthesized from cheap precursors like lemon juice and Cysteamine that offers –NH2, -OH, -SH and –COOH functional groups on its surface that play a significant role in dye adsorption along with the evolution of highly efficient photodegradation capacity of dyes in the hydrogel through ROS generation. Intermittent ring-opening of PVP with the formation of dangling carboxylic acid functionality provides intense crosslinking with CD leading to the hydrogel formation. The resultant hybrid hydrogel shows superior physico-mechanical properties than standard PVP derived hydrogels and demonstrated a sharp pH response for the sol-gel transition. As an added advantage, the PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel was found to have interesting antibacterial activity on both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, by virtue of the ROS generation ability from the embedded CD. Thus, a facile, metal-free, inexpensive, and easily scalable versatile PVP-CD hydrogel is reported that demonstrates simultaneous dye adsorption, photo-degradation, and antibacterial activity with the provision of its reuse after treatment with dilute acids up to four cycles.

The Blue fluorescence emitting CD was synthesized from lemon juice, a well-known source of citric acid in the presence of the aminothiol compound cysteamine as a source of N/S dopant in the CD by hydrothermal method (Singh et al., 2017). The purity of the prepared CD after dialysis was checked with the Reverse Phase-High Performance Liquid Chromatography (RP-HPLC) analysis that showed a sharp peak ∼8.5 min with peak purity index 0.99 indicating good homogeneity of the prepared CD (Fig. S1, SI). The powder X-ray diffractogram of CD showed a peak at ∼27.5°, corresponding to the presence of (002) planes of the graphitic framework (Fig. 1 A) (Yang et al., 2019). The Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images depicted spherical morphology of the CD with an average diameter of ∼7 nm (Fig. 1B). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of the CD showed lattice spacing of ∼0.36 nm that confirmed weak graphitic crystallinity reminiscent of insignificant interlayer π bond overlap (Fig. 1C). In the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the CD sample, occurrence of NH stretching (3183 cm−1), OH stretching (3597 cm−1), CO stretching (1710 cm−1), C–H stretching (2950 cm−1), OH bending (1390 cm−1), CO stretching (1176 cm−1), and CN stretching (1126 cm−1) indicated the presence of -NH2, −COOH, and −OH groups on the surface of the CD (Fig. 1D). For comparison, we also prepared CD from pure citric acid and cysteamine that showed similar graphitization in pXRD and surface functional groups in FTIR as CD derived from lemon juice, making it standardized and easy to reproduce the results (Fig. S2, SI).

In the UV–vis spectra of CD, a peak at 350 nm corresponding to n-π* transition of lone pair of electrons present on CO and –NH2 groups were obtained (Fig. 1E). The carbonyl and amine groups act as auxochromes, as observed in UV–vis Spectra. The lone pair of electrons available on these auxochromes are the nonbonding electrons, which are available for transition to the pi* orbitals (unsaturated bond in the graphitic layer of CD) corresponding to absorption at 350 nm (λmax) (Li et al., 2018; Mandal et al., 2018). Steady-state fluorescence emission spectra showed excitation dependent fluorescence emission where a redshift was evident in the region of 400–435 nm, with a change in excitation wavelength from 345 to 365 nm (Fig. 1F). However, the strongest emission was obtained at 427 nm for the CD when excited at 350 nm.

Carboxylation of PVP was performed as a strategy for the functionalization of PVP for its subsequent conjugation to the CD. The ring-opening of the pyrrolidone moiety of PVP was achieved through refluxing in NaOH with simultaneous methylation of the γ-amino butyric position of the PVP in a reductive environment to prevent ring closure (Baganizi et al., 2017). The preparation of carboxylated-PVP was confirmed by 1H NMR and FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 and S3, SI). The emergence of a new peak at 1.93 ppm corresponding to the methyl group in the 1H NMR spectra of carboxylated PVP indicates a successful carboxylation of greater than 15 % of the PVP substrate (Fig. 2 and Table S1, SI).

Carboxylated PVP was further conjugated to CD using EDC/NHS mediated coupling where pendant amino groups of the CD are conjugated to carboxylic acid groups of PVP through amide linkages (Scheme S1, SI). PVP-CD conjugate was purified by dialysis to eliminate unreacted CD, EDC, and NHS. Successful conjugation of PVP with CD was confirmed from the advent of a new peak at 4.8 ppm corresponding to amide hydrogen in the 1H NMR spectra.

In FTIR of PVP, a characteristic peak at 1643 cm−1 for CO bond stretching of the pyrrolidine ring was observed along with peaks for OH stretching (broad peak at ∼ 3405 cm−1), C–H stretching (2965 cm−1), OH bending (1300−1420 cm−1) and C–H bending (1260−1100 cm−1). After the carboxylation of PVP, the characteristic CO stretching peak was shifted to 1675 cm−1 that indicates the ring-opening of the pyrrolidone ring (Baganizi et al., 2017), with a large, broad peak between 2700 to 3600 cm−1 representing OH, NH, and C–H stretching modes. Following covalent conjugation of carboxylated PVP with CD, characteristics peaks at 1663 cm-1, and 1710 cm−1 was obtained for CO stretching of pyrrolidone moiety and carboxylic group of CD respectively. Furthermore, the significant, broad peaks between 2500 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 contain the characteristic peaks C–H stretching (2893 cm−1), and double-humped peaks for OH and NH stretching of NH2 (3005 and 3142 cm−1) groups of CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: A calculated amount of water was added to the purified PVP-CD conjugate and equilibrated to obtain the PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel, where CD essentially acts as a crosslinker. As a control, the hydrogel was also created from carboxylated PVP devoid of any CD and with CD loosely added to carboxylated-PVP without any covalent conjugation. The rheological behavior of the hydrogels was investigated by evaluating the storage modulus (G’) and the loss modulus (G”) as a function of frequency, which characterize the elasticity, rigidity, and plasticity of the hydrogels (Fig. 3 A) (Alsarra et al., 2011). In both carboxylated-PVP containing the free CD and PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel, G’ was higher than G” suggesting elastic behavior of the hydrogels. However, higher G’ value of PVP-CD hydrogel than the carboxylated-PVP mixed with non-covalently associated CD indicates that the strength of the PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel is higher due to the extensive cross-linking initiated by the conjugated CD. The elasticity of carboxylated-PVP was found to be significantly less than the CD infused and PVP-CD hydrogels (Fig. S4, SI). The PVP-CD conjugate appeared as granulated pale yellowish-white solid that swell appreciably after water addition and interestingly appear as a brilliant blue fluorescent hydrogel upon UV light exposure (Fig. 3 B–D).

The SEM image of carboxylated-PVP and PVP with free CD showed limited numbers of pores with large pore sizes obtained due to cross-linking among the open ring polymeric chain of PVP (Fig. S5, SI). However, higher amounts of pores with relatively small pore sizes were observed in PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel due to enhanced crosslinking between the open ring polymeric chains with CD that considerably increase the surface area of the hydrogel (Fig. 4 A). The specific surface area and porous nature of the PVP-CD hydrogel and Carboxylated-PVP hydrogel with free CD was determined from the Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curve of Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) gas Sorptometry measurements (Fig. 4C–D). The isotherms were identified as type IV isotherms with type H3 hysteresis loop, which is a typical characteristic of mesoporous materials (Ma et al., 2017). The calculated BET specific surface area of the carboxylated PVP hydrogel with free CD was 14.34 m2 g−1 that increased to 20.3 m2 g−1 for PVP-CD covalently conjugated hybrid hydrogel. The pore-size distributions were in the range of 1–35 nm, and the mean pore diameter of PVP-CD hydrogel and Carboxylated-PVP hydrogel with free CD was found to be ∼3.11 nm and 2.51 nm respectively. The evidently larger surface area of PVP-CD hydrogel compared to the carboxylated PVP hydrogel with loosely added CD offer an advantage to adsorb more organic dyes on their surface in the former.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye adsorption properties of PVP-CD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Cationic dyes like Malachite Green and Crystal Violet and anionic dye like Eosin Y, are well-known dyes in industries and therefore chosen as model dye pollutants to explore their adsorption on PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel. In all cases, the dye adsorption capacity of PVP-CD hydrogel was highest compared to only carboxylated-PVP or PVP hydrogel mixed with a free CD. This is a direct consequence of significant crosslinking introduced by CD in PVP-CD hydrogel resulting in innumerable pores in the hydrogel only to be filled by dyes after adsorption, as evidenced from SEM image (Fig. 4B and S5b, SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time on dye adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The PVP-CD hydrogel (50 mg) was found to adsorb ∼2 mg of the dyes in 12 h, with gradual swelling of the hydrogel (Fig. 5 A–B and S6A, SI). The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) was found to depend significantly on the contact time. Dye adsorption sharply increases initially with increasing contact time and flattens out after achieving the equilibrium indicating the adsorption is monolayer (Fig. 5C and S6B, SI). The adsorption of dye from aqueous solution using a solid adsorbent usually follows pseudo-first or second-order kinetics (Tang et al., 2012). In this case, for all the three dyes, the value of correlation coefficient (R2) was found to be greater than 0.99 for pseudo-second-order, whereas this value was less than 0.99 for the pseudo-first-order model signifying the former being operative during the adsorption process (Fig. 5D, and S6C, S7A-C, Table S2, SI). As a control, we also evaluated the dye adsorption properties of free CD in solution. The adsorption of dyes on the surface of free CD followed second-order kinetics where equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of CD towards Eosin Y was found to be higher (8.75 mg/g) compared to Malachite Green (5.75 mg/g) and Crystal Violet (5.3 mg/g), which is 3–5 times less than the PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel (Fig. S8 and Table S3, SI).

Apart from using the CD as a crosslinker, one of the purposes of infusing CD in the PVP-CD hydrogel matrix is to use it as a photocatalyst for dye degradation. The CD used herein was found to generate ROS efficiently on exposure to sunlight, which was evident from the conversion of non-fluorescent Dihydrorhodamine 123 to fluorescent Rhodamine when coincubated with CD solution for less than 5 min in sunlight (Fig. S9, SI). Malachite Green, Crystal Violet, and Eosin Y were adsorbed on PVP-CD hydrogel and found to degrade within 30 min of exposure to sunlight (Fig. 6 A–B and S10A, SI). Such degradation of the dyes was not observed on hydrogel derived from carboxylated-PVP and significantly lower in PVP hydrogel mixed with a free CD. Photoexcitation of electrons in the CD retards the electron − hole pair recombination and generates ROS that further reacts with water to form hydroxyl radical. The dye molecules adsorbed on the surface of CD in the hydrogel react with hydroxyl radicals and holes, resulting in their oxidative decomposition.

To determine the effectiveness of CD as a photocatalyst, the extent of dye degradation was quantitatively evaluated in the presence of CD only in solution. Crystal Violet was found to be degraded most (>90 %) followed by Malachite Green (>80 %) in 2.5 h, where the majority degradation happens within the first 30 min (Fig. 6C and E). However, the degradation of Eosin Y was slightly lower (>60 %) (Fig. S10B, SI).

The photoinduced dye degradation by CD followed pseudo-first-order reaction mechanism where a drastic reduction in half-life (t1/2) was evident for all the three dyes (Fig. S11D, SI). The band gap for CD was reduced to 3.16 eV after exposure to sunlight from 3.22 eV as observed from the Tauc plots ((αhυ)2 vs. hυ) of CD (Fig. S12, SI). Irradiation of the CD with photons increases the atomic vibrations that coerce the electrons on the surface functional groups of the CD. Electrons could break away from their covalent bonds (mostly C–S (bond energy ∼250 kJ/mol) and CN bonds (bond energy ∼290 kJ/mol)) after absorbing photonic energy (1 mol photonic energy E = hν/λ (if λ =480 nm) ∼ 250 kJ/mol) and move freely in the lattice. As a result, the number of electrons in the valance band increases, which subsequently increases the valance band height and reduces the band gap enhancing ROS generation. (Bhati et al., 2018; Khare et al., 2018a,b)

The plausible mechanism of degradation of dyes by the CD in the presence of sunlight was ascertained through trapping of the ROS by various radical scavengers (Zheng at al., 2017). Na2-EDTA was used to trap the surface generated holes (h+), parabenzoquinone (p-BZQ) to trap superoxide (O2−), and methanol (MeOH) for trapping hydroxyl (HO·) radicals (Fig. 6D and 6 F, S10C, SI). The decrease in the photodegradation process in the presence of a particular radical scavenger indicates the presence of that radical and its vital role in dye degradation. In the presence of Na2-EDTA and CD, the degradation of the dyes was reduced by ∼30–35 %, and ∼20–25 % with MeOH. However, in the presence of p-BZQ, the photodegradation of all the dyes was increased by ∼2-5% that signifies the greater number of holes being created on the surface of CD (Fig. S13–15, SI). Upon sunlight irradiation, electron-hole pairs are formed by the transfer of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band of the CD. These Excited electrons can recombine with holes through direct and surface recombination, which results in reduced photocatalytic efficiency (Liu et al., 2017). Since p-BZQ is an O2
− scavenger, its presence inhibits the recombination process. Thus, more holes are created on the surface of CD that ultimately enhances the dye degradation proficiency. In the presence of the OH· quencher MeOH, the efficiency of dye degradation decreases. Further decrease in dye degradation efficiency was observed in the presence of the hole quencher Na2-EDTA, which indicates that irradiation of CD predominantly forms holes that initiate the dye degradation (Scheme S2, SI) (Bhati et al., 2018). A comparative proton 1H NMR analysis of all three dyes and CD showed complete photodegradation of the aromatic rings after incubation with CD in sunlight (S16-S20, SI).

Regeneration analysis of Hydrogel was performed to confirm the reusability of PVP-CD hydrogel by the collection of residual PVP-CD hydrogel and repeated washing (3–4 times) with 0.01 M HCl solution followed by washing with distilled water until the pH of the effluent became neutral. The regenerated sample was then dried in the oven at 80 °C for reuse. Dye degradation efficiency decreased significantly after four photocatalytic cycles, mostly due to the inability of the washing step to remove residual degraded dye products that forms a sticky residue on the surface of the CD inhibiting further dye adsorption and ROS generation (S21, SI).

The antibacterial activity was measured against pure cultures of gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) independently after 10 min of sunlight exposure in the presence of PVP-CD hydrogel and free CD. Cells grown to mid-log phase were maintained in PBS containing Triton-X-100 surfactant for easy recovery of suspended cells from the swollen hydrogel after irradiation with sunlight. On exposure to sunlight, only ∼0.3 log killing of untreated S. aureus bacterial cells, used as a control, was observed. However, in the presence of PVP-CD and CD, the bacterial killing increased to ∼3.7 log and ∼4.7 log compared to the untreated control and ∼2.2 log compared to PVP control, respectively (Fig. 7 A–B and S22, SI). We found ∼2.2 log more killing with PVP-CD hydrogel exposed S. aureus cells than PVP alone (P < 0.001). The photo-antibacterial activity of the PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel is attributed to the ROS generated from CD upon exposure to sunlight. In an obvious consequence, the photo-antibacterial activity of free CD was better than PVP-CD due to better contact of CD with bacterial cells than the swollen PVP-CD hydrogel. Similar antibacterial activity of PVP-CD hydrogel and CD was observed for E. Coli cells (Fig. 7C and S23, SI). It indicates bacterial elimination through CD mediated ROS generation in a hydrogel matrix is achievable irrespective of the bacterial membrane/cell wall composition due to the presence of –NH2, −OH, COOH, and –SH groups on the surface of the CD awarding zwitterionic nature to the CD.

Solar water disinfection (SODIS) is known for decades as an easy method for disinfecting water that eliminates microbial pathogens and reduces diarrhoeal morbidity, including cholera (McGuigan et al., 2012). The antimicrobial effect is attributed to the combined effect of thermal heating and UV radiation from sunlight. Gram-positive bacteria like Staphylococcus epidermidis are killed with sunlight in a shorter time than gram-negative bacteria E. coli, whereas endospores of Bacillus subtilis take more than a day (Boyle et al., 2008). The photo-antibacterial activity meant for water treatment is greatly facilitated in the presence of nanocomposites of Titanium and zinc oxides that essentially generate ROS upon exposure to sunlight (Nguyen et al., 2019). Although graphene nanosheets and CD has been reported for sunlight-induced metal-free photocatalytic degradation of pollutant dyes, to the best of our knowledge, the use of CD in wastewater treatment for simultaneous dye adsorption, photo-degradation and as photo-antibacterial agents have not been reported (Zheng et al., 2017; Khare et al., 2018a,b). Our experiments clearly demonstrate that by the time a given amount of dye in aqueous solution is photodegraded in sunlight on the PVP-CD hydrogel matrix, the percolating water is virtually free of bacteria for further relevant downstream applications. The free CD in solution showed better antibacterial activity owing to superior contact of the CD with bacterial cells. However, such an intimate interaction is not possible when CD is present in the three-dimensional network with PVP leading to a decrease in antibacterial efficacy. Nevertheless, this tradeoff is worth considering the viable formulation of the hydrogel for easy separation and reusability compared to free CD.

Efficacy of PVP-CD against real polluted system for dye adsorption, degradation, and antibacterial action, effluent from a textile industry (Hajipur Industrial area, Bihar, India) was collected that predominantly contain Rhodamine B (λmax 555 nm) and methylene blue (λmax 665 nm) along with traces of other unknown pollutants with a pH ∼8, total dissolved salt (TDS) ∼190 mg/l and total suspended solids (TSS) ∼ 1200 mg/L. The photo-induced dye degradation by CD was found to follow pseudo-first-order reaction mechanism with better efficiency for degradation of Rhodamine B. A two-stage process was required for effective elimination and degradation of the dyes by PVP-CD hydrogel, where the first step is simple filtration that removes the sludge before being introduced in the hydrogel to prevent clogging of the CD surfaces (Fig. S25 and Table S4, SI). Also, treatment of real polluted water with PVP-CD hydrogel reduces bacterial count > 2.3 log within 10 min of sunlight irradiation compared to untreated cells indicating the efficacy of disinfection against naturally occurring system and future exploration of this hydrogel as point-of-use water disinfection reagent (Fig. S26, SI, P < 0.001) (Zhang and Oyanedel-Craver, 2013; Wang et al., 2017).

The fabrication of various polymeric hydrogels for dye adsorption and photo-degradation for wastewater treatment exist in literature. However, the challenge to address sustainable formulation, efficacy for adsorption, and photodegradation irrespective of the residual charge on the dyes, biodegradability of the formulation, and simultaneous achievement of antimicrobial activity without using any metal or external reagents in the formulation is seldom achieved. The present work describes the synthesis of high-quantum-yield, environment-friendly CD from easily available precursors like lemon juice that are covalently conjugated to carboxylated-PVP to create a hydrogel formulation that precisely address the above concerns. The adsorption of dyes on the PVP-CD hydrogel was predominantly through H-bonding, inductive effect, and π-π interaction of both CD and the PVP backbone (Scheme S3, SI) (Nayab et al., 2014). Reportedly, CD has been found to photodegrade certain dyes, but a formulation to fabricate them for practical usage in water treatment has never been realized. Herein, the CD embedded in the hydrogel is endowed with multiple functions, as a crosslinker for PVP in the hydrogel, an adsorbent for dyes along with PVP, and, most importantly, a visible light excitable ROS generator. The sunlight-induced ROS was found to degrade both cationic and anionic organic dyes with high efficiency by the hole transfer mechanism. The dye adsorption and degradation were found to follow pseudo-second and pseudo-first-order kinetics, respectively. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CD was found to be replenished upon a dilute acid treatment that could be reused up to four times for the catalytic cycle without much loss of fidelity. Reportedly, photocatalytic degradation of PVP is primarily affected by the Titanium oxide-based catalyst in the presence of H2O2, both of which are absent in the hybrid hydrogel that explains the resistance of the PVP-CD hydrogel from photodegradation (Suave et al., 2018).

Moreover, systematic annihilation of both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria upon short sunlight exposure owing to the ROS generation from the CD in the hydrogel add to the versatility of the hydrogel in wastewater remediation containing dye effluents. The experimental set up is simple and easy, which shows the PVP-CD hydrogel-based filtration in open air with promise for facile scale up operations (Fig. S24, SI). Furthermore, a comparison between CD-based methods reported elsewhere for dye adsorption and degradation with our method shows a significant improvement over past strategies, the most important being a workable formulation for practical usage (Table S5, SI) (Bhati et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2017; Muthulingam et al., 2015; Saud et al., 2015; Cheng et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2014). We envisage substantial development in simultaneous elimination of dyes, heavy metals, and microbes from wastewater in the future with one-for-all sustainable hybrid hydrogel formulations like the one reported here that would explore the multifaceted applications of CD.

PVP (K-90), Formaldehyde, Cysteamine, methanol, Benzoquinone, NaBH4, NaOH, EDTA, Malachite green oxalate (C₅₂H₅₄N₄O₁₂), Crystal Violet Chloride (C25N3H30Cl), Eosin Y (C20H6Br4Na2O5) were purchased from Merck. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Millipore water was used for preparing solutions for spectroscopy. Ordinary tap water was used for dye adsorption and degradation experiments.

The blue emitting CD with –NH2, −COOH and –SH groups was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Lemon juice (10 mL, pulp-free) and cysteamine (8.7 mmol, 0.64 g) was taken in a beaker (25 ml) and evaporated at 90 °C overnight. The resultant yellowish viscous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 ͦ C for 6 h. The sample was diluted with 50 ml water followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for removing the large carbonized particles. The yellowish filtrate containing the CD was collected and filtered through a 0.2 μm syringe filter for further purification and stored at 4 °C for downstream use. CD from pure citric acid (10 mmol, 2.1 g) and cysteamine (8.7 mmol, 0.64 g) were prepared by following a similar procedure as above.

The ring-opening of PVP was done by treating the PVP (0.2 g) in 10 ml, 0.5 (N) NaOH at 140 °C under reflux condition for 48 h. The γ-amino butyric position of the PVP was methylated in-situ by treating the reaction mixture with 600 u l (35 %) formaldehyde at pH 9 and 0 °C for 1 h. NaBH4 (1.5 %) was added to the reaction mixture and stirred for 1.5 h at room temperature (Baganizi et al., 2017). After the carboxylation, the solution was dialyzed for 6 h to remove the unreacted NaBH4, formaldehyde, and NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of carboxylated PVP with CD
ce:section.ce:para: The carboxylated-PVP and CD with pendant –NH2 groups were covalently conjugated by using EDC/NHS conjugation method. Carboxylated PVP (1 mg/mL, 10 mL) was mixed with 1 ml of EDC (500 mg/mL) solution in 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer (10 mM, pH 6) and stirred for 1 h at room temperature followed by addition of NHS (1 mL, 500 mg/mL) at neutral pH, and stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Unbound CD was removed by dialysis in water overnight (18 h) to obtain PVP-CD conjugated product. The product was concentrated under vacuum at 60 ͦ C to an off-white solid. A calculated amount of water was added to the solid to create the hydrogel and incubated in an air oven (around 75 ͦ C) until a constant weight of the hydrogel was attained. Hydrogel from only carboxylated PVP was made in a similar way except for the addition of CD and EDC/NHS coupling step.

Powder XRD analysis of the CD was done with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54°A) X-ray source on a Rigaku TTRX-III instrument. TEM analysis was done with a diluted sample applied on a carbon-coated copper TEM grid, and images were acquired on a Philips CM-200 operating at 100 Kv voltage. HRTEM images were acquired on a Tecnai G2, F30 Transmission Electron Microscope operating at 300 Kv voltage and 100, 000X magnification. FT-IR was done using KBr pellet in a Perkin Elmer Spectrum-400 instrument within the range of 1000-4000 cm−1 transmittance. RP-HPLC (controller; CBM-20Alite, pump; LC-20AD, PDA detector; SPD-M20A; Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan.) of CD solution was performed in a reverse phase-HPLC system using C18 G (250 × 4.6 mm) column using water-methanol-acetonitrile as eluent. Photophysical studies were performed by using UV–vis absorption spectra (UV-2550 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (Fluoromax-4 P spectrofluorometer, Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan) at room temperature and water as a solvent unless otherwise mentioned.

1H NMR was done on a BRUKER NMR AVANCE-400 MHz for characterization of the PVP, Carboxylated-PVP, and PVP-CD conjugate in CD in CDCl3 solvent for NMR analysis. In the case of photodegraded dyes, D2O and DMSO were used as an NMR solvent to make solutions from the vacuum dried degraded dye products.

The rheological studies of hydrogel were carried out on a rheometer (Anton Paar, MCR 302, Austria). The hydrogel samples were prepared as 2 mm thick disks with diameters equal to the P20 TiL plate’s diameter. The tests were performed by sweeping the frequency from 0.1–100 rad. s-1, at a constant strain of 1% at room temperature.

Field Emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were obtained from a Gemini SEM 500 microscope (ZEISS). The freeze-dried hydrogel samples were coated with a thin film of gold for effectual imaging before being charged.

BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) Surface area analysis and pore size distribution experiment were done using Quanta chrome Autosorb iQ2 Analyser with liquid N2 at 77 K. Before the experiment, the degassing of the samples was done at 100 °C for 5–6 h.

The dye adsorption capacity of carboxylated-PVP and PVP-CD conjugate were studied by using crystal violet, malachite green, and Eosin Y dyes. The adsorption kinetics experiments were studied by taking a 15 ml dye solution (100 mg/l in a 20 ml glass sample vial), followed by the addition of 50 mg of the hydrogel. The concentration of the dye solution was measured spectrophotometrically at a time interval of 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24 h for kinetics studies by withdrawing aliquots of the dye solution. The amounts of dye adsorbed on the surface of the hydrogel were calculated using the following equation.
(1)
Q e =
V ( C 0 - C e )
m
Where C0 and Ce are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of dye solutions (mg/L) respectively, V represents the volume of solution (mL), and m is the oven-dry mass of prepared hydrogel (g).

The pseudo-first-order equation is represented by
(2)
1
Q t
= (
K 1
Q 1
) (

) +
1
Q 1

And the pseudo-second-order reaction is represented by
(3)

1
Q 2
t
Where Qt expressed the amount of dye adsorbed on the hydrogel at time t, Q1 (mg g−1) was the maximum adsorption capacity in the pseudo-first-order case, Q2 (mg g−1) was the maximum adsorption capacity for pseudo-second-order adsorption, K1 (h−1) and K2 (g. mg−1 h−1) was the pseudo-first and second-order rate constant respectively. The value of K1 obtained by plotting 1/Qt with1/t, and the value of K2 for the pseudo-second-order reaction was obtained from the plot of t/Qt vs. t.

The photocatalytic activity of CD (50 μL, 105 mg/ml) was measured by adding it to 10 ml (20 mg/L) of dyes after stirring the dye solutions for 30 min in the dark to allow it to reach adsorption and desorption equilibration. The resulting solutions were then exposed to sunlight, and 1 ml aliquots were withdrawn at 30 min time intervals for UV–vis spectroscopic analysis of photocatalytic degradation after centrifugation. The same experiment was done with a real pollutant system (Textile dye effluent) that mainly contains Rhodamine B and methylene blue dye.

Radical scavenging experiments to present mechanism of dye degradation by CD were performed using three types of scavengers, viz. disodium ethylene diamminetetraacetate (1 mM, Na2-EDTA) for the trapping of the surface generated holes (h+); p-BZQ (1 mM) for the trapping of superoxide (O2−); and MeOH (1 mM) for the trapping of hydroxyl (HO·) radicals.

ROS generation by the CD (2.5 ml, 0.05 mg/ml) was determined by evaluating the band gap from Tauc plots ((αhυ)2 vs. hυ) obtained from UV–vis spectroscopic data of CD solution irradiated with sunlight (∼11 AM, IST, September 2019, clear sky) for 30 min. The ROS generation efficiently of CD was also examined by using Dihydrorhodamine123, which was evident from the conversion of non-fluorescent Dihydrorhodamine123 to fluorescent Rhodamine when coincubated with CD solution for less than 5 min in sunlight (Mandal et al., 2019).

The antibacterial activity of CD and PVP-CD hybrid hydrogel was determined by measuring colony-forming unit (CFU) of gram-positive bacteria S. aureus ATCC 25923 and gram-negative E. coli DH5α after hydrogel treatment in presence and absence of sunlight (Mandal et al., 2019). Cells were grown from a single colony in tryptic soya broth (TSB for S. aureus) or LB (for E. coli) till the OD600 value reaches∼1.0 (∼108 CFU/ml). Cells were pelleted, washed, and adjusted to 1.5 × 107 CFU/ml (for S. aureus) and 7 × 107 CFU/ml (for E coli) in sterile PBS containing 0.1 % Triton-X-100. Cells are transferred in centrifuged tubes containing PVP hydrogel (50 mg), PVP-CD hydrogel (50 mg), and CD (25 μl). The suspension was either kept in the dark or exposed to sunlight for 10 min with occasional mixing. The CD-Hydrogel untreated cells were used as control. After 10 min, cells were taken and serially diluted (101 to 104) in PBS, and 0.1 ml of diluted cells were plated in Tryptic Soya Agar (TSA) or LB agar plates in triplicate for S. aureus and E. coli cells respectively. In order to test the ability of bacterial disinfection of hydrogel against the real polluted system, effluent from the textile industry (Bihar, India) was collected and diluted 1:10 in sterile PBS and then treated as described above. Cells were plated in Nutrient Broth agar (Peptone 10 g/lit, beef extract 10 g/lit, NaCl 5 g/lit, and agar 15 g/lit) plates. Agar plates were further incubated for 24 h at 37℃. CFU was counted from untreated cells and compared with CFU for hydrogel-treated cells and presented in Log10 scale. Error bars were generated by calculating standard deviation from the average of three data points, and statistical significance between samples was evaluated by performing unpaired t-tests. Data were considered statistically significant and highly significant when P < 0.05 and P < 0.001, respectively (*P < 0.05; **0.05 < P < 0.01; ***0.01 < P < 0.001).

Suman Nayak: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Surendra Rajit Prasad: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Debabrata Mandal: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Prolay Das: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Validation.
